ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for ginger and onion chicken; closest parent 'chicken dish' (FOODON_03302757) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Chicken meat, cooked) used as closest parent; no specific ginger-onion chicken entry exists in FoodEx2.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of ginger and onion chicken is approximately 300 g, comprising bone-in or boneless chicken pieces coated in a ginger-scallion sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken, cooked with soy sauce and aromatics — adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, stewed (FDC ID 171477) and HPB Singapore Foodo reference values for Chinese braised chicken
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base macros derived from USDA SR Legacy stewed chicken (meat and skin, FDC ID 171477). Sodium and condiment contribution adjusted upward to reflect soy sauce, oyster sauce, and sesame oil typical of Chinese ginger-onion chicken preparation, consistent with HPB Singapore Foodo values for similar Chinese braised chicken dishes. Serving size set at 300 g per typical Singapore restaurant portion.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — meat and protein-dominant mixed dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for ginger and onion chicken. GI estimated at 20 based on analogous protein-dominant Chinese chicken dishes with minimal carbohydrate content (primarily from soy sauce and small amounts of aromatics). Protein-dominant dishes with low carbohydrate load consistently yield GI values in the 15–25 range per University of Sydney GI Database entries for similar preparations. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (20 × 2.8 × 3) / 100 = 1.68, rounded to 1.7.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150 g or request reduced-sauce preparation, as a 300 g serving provides approximately 1440 mg sodium, representing 60–72% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000–2400 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ginger and onion chicken is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI (estimated 20) and low carbohydrate content (2.8 g per 100 g), resulting in a negligible glycemic load of approximately 1.7 per serving; however, sodium from condiments should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and sodium from skin and condiments
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose skinless chicken portions and request reduced soy sauce to lower saturated fat (3.1 g per 100 g) and sodium intake, as the combination of these factors elevates cardiovascular risk when consumed in large portions regularly.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ginger and Onion Chicken (姜葱鸡, Jiang Cong Ji) is a classic Chinese dish widely served in Singapore's Chinese restaurants and zi char stalls. The dish consists of chicken pieces — typically bone-in thighs, drumsticks, or a whole cut-up bird — cooked with generous amounts of fresh ginger, spring onions (scallions), garlic, soy sauce, oyster sauce, and sesame oil. The preparation method varies by establishment: the chicken may be braised in the aromatics and sauce, stir-fried at high heat, or steamed and then finished with a hot aromatic oil pour. The result is tender, juicy chicken with a fragrant, savoury ginger-forward flavour profile, a glossy sauce, and a mild sweetness from the onions. Nutritionally, the dish is a good source of protein (approximately 18.5 g per 100 g) and provides moderate fat (11.2 g per 100 g), primarily from the chicken skin and sesame oil. Carbohydrate content is low at 2.8 g per 100 g, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. The primary nutritional concern is sodium, which can reach 480 mg per 100 g due to soy sauce and oyster sauce, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish has a low glycemic index (estimated 20) and is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Ginger provides bioactive gingerols with anti-inflammatory properties. The dish is commonly served with steamed white rice in Singapore and is a staple of Chinese family-style dining.
common_user_queries: How many calories in ginger and onion chicken?
Is ginger chicken suitable for diabetics?
Is ginger scallion chicken high in sodium?
Can I eat ginger onion chicken if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of Chinese chicken dishes?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for ginger and onion chicken was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values were adapted from USDA SR Legacy stewed chicken (FDC ID 171477) with sodium adjusted upward to reflect condiment contribution typical of this dish. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry used for this adaptation. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary source for this dish preparation. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, and selenium values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 171477 and are flagged as adapted. Sodium value of 480 mg per 100 g reflects condiment-adjusted estimate for restaurant preparation. GI of 20 is an analogous estimate for a protein-dominant Chinese chicken dish with low carbohydrate content, based on University of Sydney GI Database reference range for similar preparations. The food_name in the input JSON contained a trailing parenthesis ('Ginger & Onion Chicken)') which has been corrected to 'Ginger and Onion Chicken'. Cuisine tags in the input (Crab, Lobster) appear to be restaurant-level tags from Riverfront Seafood and are not relevant to this specific dish; they have been excluded from the output.
